title: Where to Get Prescription Glasses in Canada: All Your Options
description: Compare optical stores, online retailers, big-box stores, and optometrist offices for buying prescription glasses in Canada. Pros, cons, and pricing for each.
tldr: You can buy prescription glasses at independent optical stores, chain retailers (LensCrafters, Hakim Optical), online (Clearly, Zenni), big-box stores (Costco, Walmart), or directly from your optometrist's office. Independent stores offer the best fitting expertise and brand selection. Online is cheapest for simple prescriptions. Big-box stores balance price and convenience. Your choice depends on prescription complexity, budget, and how much fitting expertise you need.

tldrStats.label: Types of places to buy glasses

definition.text: Businesses that sell prescription eyeglasses, including independent optical stores, chain optical retailers, online eyewear companies, big-box store optical departments, and optometrist-affiliated dispensaries. Each offers different tradeoffs in price, selection, service, and fitting quality.

sections.content:
Locally owned optical shops run by licensed opticians, often with an in-house optometrist for eye exams.

Pros:

  • Most personalized service and fitting expertise
  • Widest selection of designer and niche brands
  • In-house labs for same-day glasses at some stores
  • Best choice for complex prescriptions (high Rx, progressives)
  • Ongoing free adjustments and after-care
  • Direct insurance billing at most stores

Cons:

  • Generally higher prices than online or big-box
  • Selection varies by store

Best for: Progressive lenses, strong prescriptions, first-time glasses wearers, anyone who values expert fitting and personal service. Stores like Charm Optical in Edmonton carry 500+ frames and provide the kind of one-on-one fitting that makes a real difference in how your glasses perform.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

National and regional chains like LensCrafters, Hakim Optical, FYidoctors, and Specsavers.

Pros:

  • Consistent experience across locations
  • Often have promotions and bundle deals
  • Most have in-house optometrists for one-stop shopping
  • Insurance direct billing widely supported

Cons:

  • Staff may prioritize sales volume over personalized service
  • Brand selection tends toward the parent company's portfolio (especially LensCrafters/EssilorLuxottica)
  • Less flexibility on pricing and custom requests

Best for: People who want a predictable experience, prefer one-stop shopping (exam + glasses), or want to take advantage of chain-specific promotions.

Companies like Clearly, Zenni Optical, Warby Parker, and EyeBuyDirect.

Pros:

  • Lowest prices — complete glasses starting under $50
  • Huge selection to browse from home
  • Virtual try-on tools
  • Convenient for reordering or backup pairs

Cons:

  • No professional fitting — you measure your own PD and hope it's accurate
  • Can't try frames on physically before buying
  • Returns can be complicated
  • Progressive lens quality varies significantly
  • No free adjustments — if frames don't fit, your options are limited

Best for: Simple single vision prescriptions, backup pairs, budget-conscious buyers who know their measurements and frame preferences. Not ideal for first-time wearers, progressive lenses, or prescriptions above +/-4.00.

Costco Optical, Walmart Vision Centre, Real Canadian Superstore.

Pros:

  • Competitive pricing — often 20-40% below independent stores
  • Access to brand-name frames (though selection varies)
  • Licensed opticians on staff
  • Costco in particular has strong lens quality and pricing

Cons:

  • Less time spent on fitting per customer (higher volume)
  • Smaller frame selection than specialized optical stores
  • Wait times can be longer
  • May not carry specialty or luxury brands

Best for: Value-conscious buyers who want decent quality without top-tier service. Costco is especially good for standard prescriptions with quality lenses at below-market prices.

sections.h2: How to Choose the Right Place for You
sections.content:
Your SituationBest OptionWhy
First pair of glassesIndependent optical storeProfessional fitting is essential for first-timers
Progressive lensesIndependent or quality chainFitting precision critical for progressive corridors
Simple single vision, tight budgetOnline or CostcoLess fitting-sensitive, price matters most
Designer brandsIndependent optical storeWidest brand selection and expert styling advice
Backup/second pairOnlineYou know your preferences, just need affordable backup
Kids' glassesIndependent storeGrowing faces need expert fitting and durable frames

Many people use a combination: primary glasses from a local optical store with professional fitting, and a backup pair from an online retailer. This gives you the best of both worlds — quality primary glasses and an affordable spare.

If you're in Edmonton, Charm Optical offers eye exams, a selection of 500+ frames from brands like Ray-Ban and Oakley, same-day single vision lenses, and direct insurance billing — all in one visit.

sections.h2: Insurance Coverage for Glasses in Canada
sections.content:
Most Canadians with employer-sponsored health benefits have optical coverage that helps pay for glasses. Typical plans cover $200-$500 every 1-2 years toward frames and lenses combined. Some plans separate frame and lens allowances, while others combine them into a single optical benefit. The specifics depend on your employer's plan — always check your benefit booklet or call your insurer before shopping.

In Alberta, common insurance providers for optical coverage include Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and Great-West Life. Government programs like AISH and Alberta Works also provide optical benefits for eligible residents. Children under 18 in Alberta can access basic optical coverage through the Alberta Child Health Benefit.

The easiest way to use your benefits is through direct billing — the optical store submits the claim to your insurer electronically, and you only pay the remaining balance at the counter. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers direct insurance billing for most Alberta insurance providers, including Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins. This means no out-of-pocket costs up front and no paperwork to submit later. book an eye exam online to get your prescription updated and use your benefits before they reset.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What is the cheapest place to buy prescription glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Online retailers like Zenni and EyeBuyDirect offer the lowest starting prices (under $50 complete). In physical stores, Costco Optical typically has the best pricing. The tradeoff with cheaper options is less personalized fitting and limited after-care.

faqs.q: Can I buy glasses without going to an optician?
faqs.a: You can buy frames online and have lenses made remotely if you have a current prescription and know your PD. However, you'll miss professional fitting, which affects comfort, visual accuracy, and lens longevity. For simple prescriptions it's workable; for progressives or strong Rx it's risky.

faqs.q: Is it better to buy glasses at the eye doctor's office?
faqs.a: Optometrist offices offer convenience (exam + glasses in one visit) and their opticians understand your prescription well. However, they sometimes have smaller frame selections and less competitive pricing than independent optical stores. It depends on the specific office.

Costco Optical offers good quality lenses (often Essilor brand) at competitive prices, with licensed opticians on staff. Frame selection is more limited than specialized optical stores, and the higher-volume environment means less personalized fitting time. For standard prescriptions, it's excellent value.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Canada?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans in Canada include optical coverage of $200-$500 every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical in Edmonton direct-bills most Alberta insurers — you only pay the difference.

faqs.q: Where is the best place to buy glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames, offers same-day glasses, direct insurance billing, and professional fitting. They carry Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, and more.
